skill.name: performance-analysis
skill.description: Analyzes Claude Flow swarm performance, detects bottlenecks, and recommends targeted optimization steps.
skill.summary: Detects Claude Flow performance bottlenecks and turns swarm metrics into optimization guidance.
skill.author: Claude Flow Team

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command and filesystem patterns, but most are markdown examples or documentation links rather than executable skill code. The main confirmed concern is that the skill teaches users or agents to run `npx claude-flow` commands, including `--fix`, and includes a JavaScript sample using `child_process.exec`; this is legitimate for performance tooling but needs a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Documented External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users to run `npx claude-flow` commands for bottleneck detection, monitoring, reports, cache analysis, and automatic fixes. These commands appear relevant to the skill purpose, but they execute local tooling and may change swarm configuration when `--fix` is used.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous, and some include `--fix`. I did not find evidence of malicious intent, secret access, or external exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: JavaScript Example Uses child_process.exec
medium_findings.description: The custom script example imports `child_process.exec` and runs Claude Flow commands through a helper. The shown calls use hardcoded command strings, so direct command injection is not evidenced, but copying this pattern with user-controlled input would be risky.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The `exec` pattern is present in a runnable example, but the visible command strings are fixed. The risk is medium because the pattern can become unsafe if adapted with dynamic input.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Many static findings for Ruby or shell backtick execution point to markdown code fences and inline command documentation. These are formatting markers or examples, not evidence of hidden command substitution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited ranges are markdown code blocks or inline command lists. No Ruby backtick execution or shell-evaluated backtick expression is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Hidden File Findings Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The path traversal and hidden file alerts reference See Also links to related local documentation paths. They do not instruct the skill to read arbitrary files or traverse user-supplied paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a list of markdown links, including `.claude` paths and parent-directory references. There is no filesystem API call or user-controlled path construction in this section.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings point to descriptive performance-analysis text, and the network reconnaissance finding points to a report example about neural pattern loading. No cryptographic API use or network scanning behavior is evidenced.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose and sample report fields, not crypto functions or reconnaissance commands. I found no supporting context for those static classifications.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.exec in Example Script
dangerous_patterns.description: A JavaScript sample uses `exec(cmd)` to run local commands. The current example passes fixed Claude Flow commands, but this API is dangerous if command strings become dynamic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly uses `child_process.exec`. The surrounding sample limits use to hardcoded commands, reducing severity but not eliminating the dangerous pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Fix Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples recommend `--fix`, which can apply automatic optimizations. This matches the skill purpose but should be reviewed before use in production systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The automatic-fix behavior is clearly documented. The exact downstream changes depend on Claude Flow, so this is a controlled operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Claude Flow swarms can slow down when agents, memory, or coordination patterns become inefficient. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through bottleneck detection, profiling, reporting, and optimization planning.
actual_capabilities: Guides bottleneck detection for Claude Flow swarms using documented CLI commands.
Explains performance metrics for communication, processing, memory, network, and cache behavior.
Shows how to generate markdown, HTML, and JSON performance reports.
Provides examples for real-time monitoring and CI performance checks.
Recommends optimization actions for topology, caching, concurrency, priority, and resources.
limitations: Requires Claude Flow tooling and access to relevant swarm metrics.
Does not include executable helper files beyond documentation examples.
Automatic fixes should be reviewed before use in production environments.
Report quality depends on the completeness and accuracy of collected metrics.

use_cases.title: Find Swarm Bottlenecks
use_cases.description: Identify slow communication, overloaded agents, memory delays, and cache problems in active Claude Flow work.

use_cases.title: Create Performance Reports
use_cases.description: Generate clear performance reports for weekly reviews, release checks, or incident follow-up.

use_cases.title: Add CI Performance Gates
use_cases.description: Run performance checks in CI and capture reports as build artifacts for regression tracking.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Bottleneck Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the performance-analysis skill to check the current Claude Flow swarm for bottlenecks. Summarize the highest-impact issue and one safe next step.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Performance Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the performance-analysis skill to create a markdown performance report for the last 24 hours. Include metrics, bottlenecks, and prioritized recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Swarm Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the performance-analysis skill to compare the current swarm with swarm-123. Highlight regressions, improvements, and likely causes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CI Performance Gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the performance-analysis skill to design a CI workflow that generates JSON performance reports, detects bottlenecks above 15 percent, and preserves artifacts.

output_examples.input: Analyze the current swarm for bottlenecks.
output_examples.output: A concise bottleneck report with affected components, estimated impact, and recommended fixes ranked by urgency.
A structured report covering executive summary, key metrics, bottleneck analysis, trends, and next actions.

output_examples.input: Help me add performance checks to CI.
output_examples.output: A CI plan that runs Claude Flow analysis commands, applies thresholds, and stores performance artifacts for review.

best_practices: Review detected bottlenecks before applying automatic fixes.
Use consistent time ranges when comparing performance reports.
Store reports as artifacts so regressions can be tracked over time.
anti_patterns: Do not run automatic fixes on production swarms without approval.
Do not compare reports from different time ranges without noting the difference.
Do not treat one metric as conclusive without checking related agent and cache data.

faq.question: What systems does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets Claude Flow swarms and workflows that expose performance metrics through Claude Flow commands.

faq.question: Can it fix bottlenecks automatically?
faq.answer: It documents commands that can request automatic fixes, but users should review planned changes first.

faq.question: Does it require Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. It supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, but Claude Flow tooling must be available.

faq.question: What report formats are covered?
faq.answer: The skill documents markdown, HTML, and JSON reports for different review and automation workflows.

faq.question: Can it be used in CI?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a CI workflow pattern for generating reports, checking thresholds, and uploading artifacts.

faq.question: What are the main safety concerns?
faq.answer: The skill includes local command execution examples and automatic fix options, so commands should be reviewed before running.
